hero.title: 4 Days Classic Yogyakarta
hero.intro: The definitive Yogyakarta journey — both UNESCO World Heritage temples in one itinerary. Visit Prambanan's towering Hindu spires at sunset from Ijo Temple, explore Borobudur's vast Buddhist terraces, and immerse yourself in royal Javanese culture through the Sultan's Palace, Taman Sari Water Castle, ancient markets, and Kasongan pottery village. The classic Jogja experience from RM1,488 per person, 100% private.
hero.heroImageAlt: Prambanan Hindu temple complex at sunset, Yogyakarta

quickFacts.saving: Save RM400 per couple with group of 3+

overview.heading: Both UNESCO Temples — Borobudur & Prambanan — in One Classic Itinerary
body: Yogyakarta sits at the intersection of Java's two greatest civilisations: the Buddhist Sailendra dynasty that built Borobudur in the 9th century, and the Hindu Mataram kingdom that erected Prambanan's soaring spires around the same era. Visiting both UNESCO World Heritage Sites in the same trip is the ultimate Yogyakarta experience — and this classic 4-day itinerary does exactly that, while also covering the royal culture, traditional crafts, ancient markets and colonial fortifications that make Jogja one of Southeast Asia's most rewarding city destinations.

On Day 1 you arrive straight into history with a Prambanan temple visit followed by sunset viewing from Ijo Temple — the highest temple in Yogyakarta — with its breathtaking panorama over the plains. Day 2 centres on Borobudur and the royal heart of the city: Kraton Palace, Taman Sari Water Castle and Kotagede silver village. Day 3 is dedicated to exploring Yogyakarta's urban heritage: museums, a Dutch colonial fort, a traditional batik market, and the hands-on pottery village of Kasongan. A leisurely Day 4 departure wraps up this comprehensive Jogja journey.

highlights.heading: What Makes This Classic Tour Special

items.title: Prambanan — Indonesia's Most Beautiful Hindu Temple
items.body: The Prambanan temple compound is the largest Hindu archaeological site in Indonesia and the second largest in Southeast Asia after Angkor Wat. Built around 850 CE by the Hindu Mataram kingdom, the complex's 47 surviving temples cluster around three towering central shrines dedicated to the Trimurti: Brahma, Vishnu and Shiva. The 47-metre Shiva temple is the tallest, its ornate volcanic stone reliefs illustrating the Ramayana epic in extraordinary detail. Like Borobudur, Prambanan is a UNESCO World Heritage Site.

items.title: Ijo Temple — Sunset from Yogyakarta's Highest Point
items.body: Perched on Gumuk Ijo hill at 410 metres above sea level, Candi Ijo is the highest temple complex in Yogyakarta. Its 11 terraced platforms command a spectacular 360-degree panorama: Prambanan's spires to the east, Mount Merapi's cone to the north, and the Indian Ocean glittering to the south. Arriving at Ijo Temple for the late afternoon light after Prambanan delivers one of Yogyakarta's most magical experiences.
items.imageAlt: Ijo Temple at sunset with Yogyakarta panorama

items.title: Borobudur — The World's Largest Buddhist Monument
items.body: The crown jewel of any Yogyakarta visit. Built in the 9th century by the Sailendra dynasty, Borobudur's nine-tiered stone pyramid covers an area of 2,500 square metres and is decorated with 2,672 bas-relief narrative panels and 504 seated Buddha statues. Ascend to the circular upper terraces with their 72 latticed stupas for panoramic views over the misty Kedu Plain and surrounding volcanoes — a scene that has defined travellers' visions of Indonesia for centuries.
items.imageAlt: Borobudur Buddhist temple upper terraces at sunrise

items.title: Kraton Yogyakarta & Taman Sari Water Castle
items.body: The Kraton is the living heart of Javanese royal culture — a walled palace complex still occupied by the Sultan of Yogyakarta, where court traditions, batik artistry and gamelan music continue unchanged for centuries. The adjacent Taman Sari (1758) was the royal pleasure garden, featuring elaborate bathing pools, an underground mosque and a network of labyrinthine alleyways lined with batik artists' workshops. Together they form the most atmospheric cultural visit in all of Java.
items.imageAlt: Taman Sari Water Castle bathing pools, Yogyakarta

items.title: Kasongan Pottery Village — Hands-On Javanese Craft
items.body: Just 7 km south of Yogyakarta city, the village of Kasongan has been the centre of traditional Javanese terracotta craft since the 17th century. Hundreds of family workshops line the village road, producing everything from decorative pots and garden statuary to contemporary ceramic homewares. Visitors can try their hand at the potter's wheel and watch master craftspeople create complex figurative pieces — a genuinely interactive cultural experience.

days.title: Arrive Yogyakarta — Prambanan Hindu Temple Complex — Ijo Temple Sunset
days.description: Your private driver-guide meets you at Yogyakarta International Airport (YIA) and after a welcome lunch at a local restaurant, head straight to one of Asia's greatest archaeological wonders: the Prambanan temple compound. This UNESCO World Heritage Site features three towering central shrines dedicated to Shiva, Vishnu and Brahma, surrounded by dozens of smaller temples and pavilions. The outer walls of the Shiva temple (47 metres tall) are carved with over 1,000 panels illustrating the Ramayana epic — your guide will highlight the key scenes. As the afternoon light softens, continue 4 km southeast to Candi Ijo, the highest situated temple complex in the Yogyakarta region at 410 metres above sea level. The 11-platform terraced complex commands an extraordinary panoramic view: Prambanan to the east, the volcanoes to the north, the Indian Ocean glinting to the south. Watching sunset from Ijo Temple is one of Yogyakarta's most memorable experiences and a perfect way to begin your Java journey. Return to the hotel for dinner and check-in.
suggestedActivities: Hire a Prambanan guide for detailed Ramayana relief panel narration
Sunset panorama photography at Candi Ijo hilltop
Watch Ramayana ballet at Prambanan open-air theatre (seasonal, own expense)
Evening dinner at a Malioboro-area warung

days.title: Borobudur — Kraton Sultan's Palace — Taman Sari Water Castle — Kotagede
days.description: Rise early for a hearty hotel breakfast before setting off for Borobudur, a 1.5-hour drive northwest. The world's largest Buddhist temple rewards an unhurried visit — your guide will lead you through the ascending narrative reliefs: the base reliefs illustrating karma and rebirth, the middle terraces depicting Buddha's past lives (Jataka tales), and finally the open circular terraces with their 72 latticed bell-shaped stupas and 360-degree views across the Kedu Plain and five surrounding volcanoes. After lunch near the temple, return to Yogyakarta city for the afternoon's cultural immersion. The Kraton Yogyakarta — a walled city-within-a-city — is the official residence of the 10th Sultan of Yogyakarta and the living repository of Javanese court culture. Stroll through open pavilions (pendopo) displaying royal gamelan sets, royal regalia and Javanese shadow puppets, while court dancers may be rehearsing in a shaded corner. A short walk away, the extraordinary Taman Sari Water Castle (1758) reveals its hidden world: ornate bathing pools where royal concubines once bathed, an underground mosque carved into volcanic stone, and labyrinthine alleyways where batik artists work. Continue to Kotagede for a glimpse of Yogyakarta's finest silverwork tradition before returning for dinner.
suggestedActivities: Early morning Borobudur visit for thinner crowds and misty light
Climb all nine levels of Borobudur for the best upper-terrace views
Batik painting lesson at Taman Sari artists' community (own expense)
Commission a custom keris (ceremonial dagger) scabbard in Kotagede silver

days.title: Sonobudoyo Museum — Fort Vredeburg — Beringharjo Market — Kasongan Pottery — Malioboro
days.description: Dedicate Day 3 to the layers of Yogyakarta's urban heritage. Begin at the Sonobudoyo Museum, which houses one of Java's finest collections of Javanese cultural artefacts — from ancient Hindu-Buddhist bronzes and wayang puppet sets to Batik Solo and traditional Javanese jewellery. Across the street stands Fort Vredeburg, the Dutch colonial fortress built in 1760 to 'protect' (in reality, to watch over) the neighbouring Kraton. Today it houses a vivid diorama museum chronicling Indonesian independence from the colonial era through to the 1949 revolution. Walk to the nearby Beringharjo Market — Yogyakarta's great covered traditional market — where three floors of stalls sell fresh produce, Javanese spices, traditional jamu herbal medicines, batik fabric by the metre, vintage clothing and street food. After lunch, drive south to Kasongan pottery village where generations of Javanese families have practised terracotta craft since the 17th century. Watch master potters at the wheel, explore the outdoor display gardens crowded with terracotta sculptures and pots, and try your own hand at throwing clay. Return via Malioboro Street for a final evening of shopping for batik shirts, wayang puppets, silver jewellery and Javanese leather goods before dinner.
suggestedActivities: Browse Sonobudoyo Museum's wayang puppet and keris collections
Fort Vredeburg independence diorama — excellent for history lovers
Bargain for batik fabric by the metre at Beringharjo Market
Try a pottery wheel session at Kasongan (own expense)
Final batik and craft shopping along Malioboro Street

days.title: Free Leisure Time — Departure
days.description: Enjoy a relaxed hotel breakfast on your final morning in Yogyakarta. Use the free time to revisit any favourite spots, pick up last-minute souvenirs at Malioboro Street or the Progo market, take a batik lesson at one of the Taman Sari community workshops, or simply savour a final cup of Java coffee at a riverside café along Jalan Code. At the appropriate time, your private driver will collect you from the hotel and transfer you to Yogyakarta International Airport (YIA) for your departure flight home to Malaysia.
suggestedActivities: Final shopping at Malioboro Street and Beringharjo Market
Batik painting class at a Prawirotaman studio (own expense)
Coffee at a Malioboro rooftop café with volcano views
Visit Prambanan Sunrise independently before checkout (own arrangement)

whyBook.heading: Why Book Your Classic Yogyakarta Tour Through Dhesu
whyBook.body: The dual-UNESCO Borobudur and Prambanan itinerary is a deceptively logistically demanding tour — getting the lighting and timing right at both sites requires genuine local knowledge. Dhesu Travel's Yogyakarta specialists have been running this classic tour for over 35 years and know exactly how to sequence the day for the best possible experience.

sections.heading: Yogyakarta Classic Tour Package — Borobudur & Prambanan from Malaysia
sections.body: The Borobudur and Prambanan dual-UNESCO experience is the definitive Yogyakarta trip for first-time visitors from Malaysia. These two ancient monuments represent two of the world's greatest religious architectural achievements — built by rival dynasties within decades of each other on the same fertile Javanese plain in the 9th century CE. Remarkably, they were lost to history after the eruption of Mount Merapi drove the population east, and both were rediscovered buried under volcanic ash and jungle in the 19th century. Today, painstakingly restored by UNESCO and the Indonesian government, they welcome millions of visitors annually. Our 4-day classic package sequences the visit logically: Prambanan and Ijo Temple on Day 1 (capturing the best afternoon and sunset light at the Hindu complex), Borobudur on Day 2 (morning light is perfect at the Buddhist monument), and two full days of Yogyakarta city culture on Days 2 and 3. Group discounts are available from as few as 3 adults, making this the best-value classic Yogyakarta tour available from Kuala Lumpur.

sections.heading: Prambanan Temple — Hindu Java's Greatest Achievement
sections.body: The Prambanan temple compound was built around 850 CE during the reign of the Hindu Mataram king Rakai Pikatan, as a direct counterpart to the Buddhist Borobudur built by the rival Sailendra dynasty to the west. The central zone originally contained 240 temples, of which 47 remain standing in various states of preservation. The three main shrines — dedicated to Shiva, Brahma and Vishnu respectively — rise to 47, 33 and 33 metres respectively, their volcanic andesite stone facades covered in intricate relief carvings. The Shiva temple walls narrate the complete Ramayana epic in carved stone panels that run clockwise around the exterior, depicting scenes from Sita's abduction by the demon king Ravana to Ram's ultimate victory. These panels are considered among the finest surviving examples of ancient Hindu narrative relief sculpture in the world. Like Borobudur, Prambanan was designated a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 1991. The Ramayana Ballet performed at the open-air Trimurti Theatre within the temple compound (on selected evenings) is one of the most dramatic cultural performances in Southeast Asia — your Dhesu guide can advise on performance dates and ticket arrangements during your visit.

items.question: Do I need a visa to visit Indonesia from Malaysia?
items.answer: Malaysian passport holders enjoy visa-free entry to Indonesia for stays of up to 30 days. Simply present your valid passport at immigration on arrival — no advance application required.

items.question: Can we see the Ramayana Ballet at Prambanan?
items.answer: Yes, the Prambanan Ramayana Ballet is performed at the open-air Trimurti Theatre within the Prambanan compound on selected evenings (typically Tuesday, Thursday and Saturday). As we visit Prambanan on Day 1, your guide can advise on performance availability and help arrange tickets if scheduled. This is a personal expense add-on.

items.question: What is the difference between Borobudur and Prambanan?
items.answer: Borobudur is a Buddhist monument built by the Sailendra dynasty around 750–850 CE; its nine tiers symbolise the Buddhist cosmological journey toward Nirvana. Prambanan is a Hindu temple compound built by the rival Mataram Hindu kingdom around 850 CE, its towering spires dedicated to the Trimurti gods Shiva, Vishnu and Brahma. Both are UNESCO World Heritage Sites and are considered the greatest religious monuments of ancient Java.

items.question: What is included at Kasongan pottery village?
items.answer: The Kasongan visit includes entrance, a walk through the village workshops, and observation of artisans at work. A hands-on pottery wheel session can be arranged for an additional fee at the workshop. Finished ceramic pieces can be purchased and many workshops offer packing for transportation.

items.question: Is this package suitable for elderly travellers?
items.answer: Yes, with some adaptation. Borobudur and Prambanan have paved paths and most areas are accessible on foot at a gentle pace, though both require some stair climbing to reach the upper levels. Kasongan and Beringharjo Market are flat and easy to navigate. Please inform us of any mobility requirements so we can advise on the most comfortable routes.

items.question: Are group discounts available for small groups?
items.answer: Yes — unusually, our group discounts start from just 3 adults. A group of 3 saves RM100/person, and a group of 5 saves RM200/person at the 3-star tier. These discounts are applied automatically when you specify your group size at booking.
